# Dental Insurance Chaos: A Systemic Operational Crisis

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

James Filkins

2mo

- Report this post

Dental Insurance Is Becoming an Operational Crisis Most dental practices aren’t struggling with dentistry. They’re struggling with insurance chaos that would make Kafka blush. Verification has turned into a scavenger hunt across portals, PDFs, and phone trees that seem designed to test human endurance. Plan data in PMS systems is a mess of duplicates, wrong group numbers, and coverage rules that contradict themselves depending on which screen you’re looking at. Claims and AR are reactive by design. Claims disappear. Denials show up weeks later with “explanations” that explain nothing. AR grows like weeds. EOB posting is a patience marathon. Paper, scans, portals — all formatted like they were created by different civilizations. And the kicker: Most practices have zero insurance intelligence. No payer insights. No denial trends. No employer mix analysis. Just crossed fingers and hope. This isn’t a workflow issue. It’s an operational tax the industry has quietly accepted for far too long. Dental teams aren’t failing. The system is.

3 Comments

Like Comment

Scott Davis 2mo

- Report this comment

This is accurate, James. In our experience, dental teams aren’t struggling with care—they’re getting crushed by verification friction and payer ambiguity. The chaos feels “normal” only because practices have been forced to absorb it for years. We see AR grow when denials arrive late, EOBs are inconsistent, and no one has clean payer intelligence. Once you track denial reasons and portal behavior like an operating system, the fog starts to lift. Until then, it’s an invisible tax.

🎯- same dynamic with chiropractic and physical therapy. Structural root cause where intermediaries exempt from MLR protections are incentivized to introduce friction with the resulting operational tax and denied claims dropping to the intermediary bottom line.

Fighting and Winning Insurance Denials

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Fallen trees from an autumn storm partially block a road in rural Washington. ©️2025 Robin Monks.

### Insurance companies benefit whenever you don't fight, so here's how to fight smart.

Insurers have a very strong incentive to not pay out claims, they want to hold on to as much of the premium as possible and want to direct as much of the premiums as they can into their related family of companies to continue to keep a high overall share of margin through vertical integration (page 8 on this FTC report gives a good visual overview). A recent KFF analysis based on 2023 data found, on average, insurers denied 1-in-5 claims.

Seeing that didn't surprise me, the number of reports on denial rates and lawsuits over excessive denials has been a consistently growing drum-beat with United, Cigna, and Humana all facing lawsuits recently over excessive denials with inadequate human review.

The statistic that concerns me even more though is that consumers appealed denials less than 1% of the time. Given the tendency to err on the side of denial, and given appeals succeed almost half the time (44%), millions of claims remain uncorrected.

### Why not appeal?

As someone who's gone through several insurance appeals in my lifetime, and 3 appeals in just the last year, I get why most people don't appeal. Most times, insurance looks like it's done something. You'll often see a negotiated rate on explanation of benefit documents. For those less savvy, it may not be obvious that these negotiated rates aren't the same as a payment from the insurer.

FIG 1: An example of the "discounted rate" shown on an Aetna Explanation of Benefits summary. It may appear that Aetna paid $945.17 of the provider bill if you're not paying attention.

Seeing this, combined with the insurer possibly paying for some of the claimed items and not for others, could lead the reader to thinking they got some value from their healthcare plan and not feel the need to fight over the remaining amount. This can be especially true if the amounts are small.

FIG 2: Another example from a Guardian dental claim, showing the first two items being covered at 80% and 50%, but the third item not being covered. Seeing your insurance has paid something might convince some not to appeal.

Home Health Prior Authorization: Why Your Staff Can't Scale It — But AI Can

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Last updated: May 2026 | Read time: 8 minutes

The core answer: Home health agencies spend an average of 13 staff hours per week managing prior authorization requests — costing an estimated $34,000 per clinician annually in pure administrative overhead, according to CMS. As Medicare Advantage enrollment grows and payer utilization management tightens, manual prior authorization is no longer an operational inconvenience. It is a structural revenue risk. AI-powered prior authorization tools are reducing that burden by automating submission, tracking, and follow-up — without adding headcount.

---

### What Is Prior Authorization in Home Health — and Why Is It Getting Worse?

Prior authorization (PA) in home health is the process by which agencies must obtain payer approval before delivering skilled nursing visits, therapy services, or extended care episodes. What was once a selective checkpoint has become a routine, high-volume administrative gauntlet — and Medicare Advantage plans are the primary driver.

According to KFF analysis, Medicare Advantage insurers processed nearly 53 million prior authorization requests in 2024 — a number that has grown steadily as MA enrollment now covers the majority of new Medicare beneficiaries. Of those requests, 4.1 million (7.7%) were fully or partially denied, each requiring appeals, documentation resubmission, or care redesign.

For home health agencies serving a mixed payer population, this volume creates a compounding burden. Every PA request requires clinical documentation, payer-specific formatting, submission through varying portals or fax systems, active follow-up, and authorization tracking against live schedules. None of this generates revenue. All of it consumes staff time that could otherwise go toward patient care.

The regulatory environment is intensifying the problem further. CMS's CY 2026 Home Health Proposed Rule includes a -6.4% aggregate cut to the home health base payment rate, according to LeadingAge analysis. Agencies are being squeezed from both sides: rising administrative costs from PA volume and declining reimbursement rates from CMS. In this environment, the cost of manual prior authorization is no longer sustainable.

There’s progress here — at least on paper. Reducing prior authorization volume, improving interoperability, and increasing transparency around denials and turnaround times are all steps in the right direction. But what continues to stand out is what remains largely absent from these conversations: behavioral health, substance use disorder treatment, and mental health care. Behavioral health authorization management is not operationally comparable to many traditional medical specialties. This isn’t simply obtaining approval for an imaging study or outpatient procedure. Integrity Billing | Substance Abuse Billing Company, alongside the providers we support, we are managing concurrent reviews multiple times per week for the same patient, defending medical necessity daily, navigating step-down disputes, and addressing retro denials long after care has been rendered. The article references reductions in prior authorization requirements for orthopedic procedures, diagnostics, outpatient therapies, and pharmacy requests, yet behavioral health remains largely implied rather than directly addressed. Meanwhile, our industry continues to operate under extraordinary administrative burden despite parity laws that were intended to improve access and fairness. And while insurers report improved automation and faster approval times, provider confidence remains low. That should tell us something. Technology and interoperability may improve workflow, but they do not solve the underlying issue if behavioral health providers continue to face inconsistent medical necessity standards, shifting criteria, delayed determinations, and reimbursement uncertainty. The behavioral health industry needs operational reform. We have complied. We self-monitor. We provide outcomes, analytics, documentation, audits, and oversight. Yet providers and utilization review teams still spend hours waiting for return calls because a payer refuses to backdate an outpatient authorization. I rarely hear orthopedic groups or obstetric practices describe operating this way. Real parity will require more than APIs and automation. It w

Why Small Dermatology Practices Are Rethinking Their EHR in 2026

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

If you are running a solo or small dermatology practice in the United States, your EHR is not just a documentation tool. It is the operational backbone of everything your team does, from the moment a patient books an appointment to the moment a claim is paid.

And yet, most small practices are using systems that were never designed for them.

General-purpose EHRs built for primary care require months of customization before they can handle dermatology-specific workflows. Mid-market platforms priced for large groups carry implementation complexity and costs that a solo provider or two-provider clinic cannot absorb. And a growing number of vendors are marketing AI features that, when you actually ask to see them in a live demo, do not yet exist in the way they are being described.

Here is what we have seen from practices that switched to Edvak in the past year and what any small dermatology practice should be thinking about before signing another EHR contract.

## THE BILLING PROBLEM NO ONE TALKS ABOUT

Dermatology billing is among the most complex in outpatient specialties. A single patient encounter can involve an evaluation, a biopsy, and destruction of a premalignant lesion \ each with its own CPT code, each requiring specific clinical documentation to survive payer review. Modifier 25, applied when a significant separately identifiable evaluation is performed on the same day as a procedure, is one of the most audited modifiers in dermatology.

"A 5 to 10 percent denial rate on modifier 25 claims can represent thousands of dollars in delayed or lost revenue each month for a small practice. Billing accuracy starts with what the EHR captures at the point of care."

The problem is structural. When clinical documentation and billing live in separate systems, or when code selection is a manual step after charting, errors accumulate. A small practice collecting $80,000 per month that improves its clean claim rate by 5 percent recovers $4,000 monthly. That math matters when you are evaluating whether to stay on your current platform.

## WHAT SMALL PRACTICES ACTUALLY NEED

We evaluated the current US dermatology EHR market against the real operational needs of practices in the solo-to-five-provider range. The requirements are consistent: dermatology-specific

Independent Practice in 2026: What the MGMA Data Confirms (and What It Doesn’t Say Out Loud)

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Over the past year, nearly every independent practice leader I work with has said some version of the same thing:

“It’s not one problem. It’s everything at once.”

Staffing. Reimbursement. Payers. Technology. Burnout. Succession planning. These challenges are not new. What’s new is how tightly stacked they’ve become.

A recent MGMA Connection article, “Tradition Meets Transformation: The State of Private Medical Practice in 2025,” puts data behind what many of us already feel. It offers one of the clearest snapshots of where independent medical practices stand as we move into 2026.

The findings are sobering. But they’re also clarifying.

### What the MGMA data clearly confirms

Several insights stand out immediately:

- Nearly 7 in 10 independent practice leaders report being less optimistic about the future than they were just a few years ago
- Financial sustainability has emerged as the dominant concern, driven by declining reimbursement and rising operating costs
- Staffing challenges continue to intensify, outpacing most practices’ ability to adapt
- And despite all of this, most physicians say they would still choose independent practice again

That last point matters more than it may appear.

Independence isn’t being abandoned because it’s broken. It is being tested because maintaining it without intention is more difficult.

### What this data tells me from the trenches

Here’s what the charts don’t fully capture.

Independent practices aren’t struggling because leaders don’t want to evolve. They’re struggling because many teams are operating at full capacity just to keep things moving.

Innovation sounds great until your office manager is also covering the front desk. Technology sounds promising until no one has time to train. Strategic planning feels vital until the next payer issue lands on your desk.

What the MGMA data reflects isn’t resistance. It reflects exhaustion.

And exhaustion changes how decisions get made.

### Independence is still the preferred model — but it’s under planned

One of the most striking findings is that passing ownership to younger partners remains the most preferred transition plan. Yet, a large number of practices still lack a formal succession strategy.

That gap is risky.

### 1. Automate Time-Consuming Tasks ⏰✨

Say goodbye to mundane administrative chores! SimplePractice automates tasks like appointment reminders, superbill generation, and calendar syncing. This means less time on paperwork and more time for patient care.

### 2. Enhance Client Experience with a Seamless Client Portal 🌐💼

Empower your clients with the Client Portal! They can easily book appointments, complete paperwork, and pay invoices—all from a user-friendly interface. Improve client satisfaction with convenient, accessible services.

### 3. Simplify Payments with Effortless Processing 💳💸

Streamline your payment processes with SimplePractice. Enjoy features like AutoPay, online credit card processing, and straightforward insurance billing. Simplify financial transactions and reduce administrative hassle for both you and your clients.

### 4. Streamline Treatment Plans with Customizable Templates 🗂️📋

Create and manage treatment plans effortlessly using SimplePractice’s extensive library of specialty-based, customizable templates. Save time and ensure comprehensive, accurate documentation for every patient.

### 5. Ensure Confidential and Secure Communication 🔐💬

Protect client trust with SimplePractice’s HIPAA-compliant messaging tool. Keep all communication secure and confidential, ensuring compliance with privacy regulations and maintaining the highest standards of client confidentiality.

Unlock the full potential of your practice with SimplePractice’ s all-in-one EHR system. Discover how it can transform your workflow, enhance client satisfaction, and boost your efficiency.

# Melissa Cleary

Founder/ President - Your Dental - Digital Marketing, Consulting, Recruiting dental professionals across the US CEO/Founder - HiredSmiles - providing innovative customer service solutions in the dental industry.

Founder at Your Dental Marketer

Newnan, Georgia, United States (US)

500 connections • 13,614 followers

## About

It started chairside—gloves on, suction in hand, adrenaline high. In the ’90s, I assisted in complex oral surgeries, managed general anesthesia cases, and supported full-mouth reconstructions before “All-on-X” was mainstream. Those early years taught me what really drives dentistry: trust, precision, and the power of life-changing care. As a Treatment Coordinator, I closed 95%+ of cases by connecting clinical excellence with strategic communication. I built comprehensive treatment plans for prosthodontists and oral surgeons and helped patients commit to big decisions with confidence. But behind the scenes, I saw what most don’t: broken systems, wasted leads, overwhelmed teams, and lost revenue. So I made the shift—from working in the practice to building the systems around it. Today, I help practices scale smarter through strategy, psychology, and modern technology. As the founder of Your Dental Marketer and Your Dental Recruiter, I lead a team delivering consulting, digital marketing, recruiting, virtual staffing, and platform development (PaaS) for forward-thinking practices. Whether you’re a PPO-heavy office or a boutique FFS model, we help you streamline operations, increase case acceptance, and build systems that work. My expertise is backed by 28+ years of hands-on dental experience, a degree in Industrial-Organizational Psychology, and advanced training with: • The Ritz-Carlton (Service + Culture) • Robert Frazier’s New Patient Practicum (Case Acceptance) • Progressive Dental’s TC Bootcamp • MGE Management Consulting • Certifications: Radiographs, Venipuncture, CPR, Ergonomics I’m fluent in Dentrix, Open Dental, Eaglesoft, Ascend, Go High Level, and Dental Intel—and I use them to design smarter workflows and track the KPIs that matter. What we deliver: • High-converting PESO marketing funnels (Paid, Earned, Shared, Owned) • Custom-built tech platforms to unify ops, marketing, and reporting • Virtual teams trained your way for true integration • Strategic consulting rooted in business psychology • Recruiting for dentists, lab techs, and in-office roles with long-term fit If you’re ready to grow with intention,

content:
Optimizing RCM and Cash Flow: The CFO’s Guide to Bridging Finance and Operations in PE-Backed Healthcare

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

### Introduction: Why Revenue Cycle Management (RCM) is a CFO’s Strategic Priority

In private equity-backed healthcare, CFOs are expected to maximize EBITDA, accelerate cash flow, and integrate financial operations across multiple acquisitions. Yet, one of the biggest barriers to financial optimization in PE-backed physician groups and specialty care practices is a fragmented revenue cycle management (RCM) system.

Many newly acquired practices retain their existing billing and RCM workflows, leading to:

- Lack of real-time cash flow insights, making working capital management unpredictable
- Unoptimized revenue capture from underbilled or rejected services
- Higher claim denials caused by poor payer contract alignment
- Delayed reimbursements due to inconsistent billing processes

💡 Why This Matters for CFOs: Without a standardized, scalable RCM strategy, cash flow stagnates, revenue synergies are delayed, and PE investors become concerned about financial inefficiencies. RCM isn’t just an operational issue—it’s a financial strategy that must be led at the CFO level.

---

### The RCM Bottleneck: How Cash Flow Gets Stuck in PE-Backed Healthcare

CFOs in PE-backed healthcare often inherit a mix of legacy systems and inefficient billing practices across their acquisitions. Without standardization, the cash conversion cycle slows down, impacting liquidity and investor confidence.

### 📍 Hypothetical Example: The PE-Backed Orthopedic Group

A PE-backed orthopedic platform grows aggressively through five acquisitions in two years. Each practice continues to use different:

- RCM workflows that differ in pre-authorization and collections procedures
- Payer contracts, each with varying reimbursement structures
- Billing platforms (e.g., Athenahealth, NextGen, ModMed)

🚨 What happens next? ❌ A 40% increase in days sales outstanding (DSO), delaying cash inflows ❌ Billing errors cause a 15% increase in denials, impacting revenue realization ❌ Untracked aging receivables lead to revenue leakage

🔹 Key Takeaway: If RCM isn’t optimized at the PE platform level, cash flow unpredictability increases, EBITDA is compromised, and valuation multiples at exit decline.

---

### 5 CFO-Led Strategies for Optimizing RCM & Cash Flow

content:
Do You Have the Management Traits of Cash Pay Medical Practices that Hurdle $1 Million in Annual Revenue?

Moving past $1 million in annual cash pay revenue for medical practices is a big hurdle. Those practices that don't surpass this number typically create a nice life style business for themselves but live with financial opportunity cost year after year. Assuming effective medical skills, there is a balance of hard and soft business management traits necessary to move past the $1 million mark. From our 7 years at the epicenter of patient acquisition and being a part of over $150 million in new patient revenue for cash pay medical practices we have seen the following be the consistent set of skills necessary to move past this financial mark.

### "Hard" Management Traits - Business Mechanics

- Diversified Practice Revenue - These practices aggressively pursue related but alternative revenue streams beyond their core physician services. These revenue streams are measured separately as their own business units and have their own set of data, marketing plans, and sales processes to optimize for profitable growth. Practices without diversified revenue suffer from financial stagnation due to limited patient volume potential.
- Automation - Greater than $1mm in annual revenue requires automation to scale volume and margins challenged by time consuming administrative tasks. From marketing automation, practice management systems, and procedure related technology these practices understand the value of time and want their staff focused on patient acquisition and experience not administration. Money is made with patient engagement and expense is managed with automation.
- Patient Experience Map - In order to drive past $1mm you must focus on patient referrals and retention. These practices have defined a specific experience for each "type" of patient that starts from inception of a lead through to repeat business and referrals. Patient groups are defined by the goals of the practice regarding classifications of procedures, $'s spent at the practice, upsells and cross sells. The map is constantly evaluated and improved based on data and patient feedback. Without experience expectations set by the practice patient engagement will be average at best causing lost opportunity for repeat business and referrals.

## The Google Dataset Search allows you to find datasets to do your data wrangling and test predictive models on, such as this one on Vitória, Brasil medical appointment no-show data from over 80 neighborhoods, featured in Kaggle.

With data from over 62 000 patients and over 110 000 appointments scheduled from end 2015 to mid-2016 and occuring from April to June 2016 in over 80 neighborhoods, I first mapped the 80 neighborhoods to their 10 respective Administrative Regions to reduce the number of variables to encode. After converting the dates and calculating time from scheduling to appointment, isolating the day of the week, month of the year, and time of day, and regrouping the ages by age group classes, etc., one can start to see the impact of the various numeric and categorical variables on no-shows.

Aside from one island neighborhood with a only no-shows, the neighborhood didn't appear to make a difference.

Youth and Adult age groups had more no-shows than seniors. Those who received an SMS were more likely to have a no-show, or reschedule.

Existing conditions like diabetes, hypertension, alcoholism, etc. didn't appear to impact no-shows.

There wait time between scheduling and appointment date didn't show much of a difference in no-shows except for the same-day or walk-in appointments, which had very few no-shows.

As expected, Fridays had more no-shows than other days of the week.

Using Leonardo Ferreira's Kaggle Kernel that has a nice pipline of predictive models on this adjusted dataset, you can reach 80% accuracy with the Random Forest classifier.

Here is a link to the shared Google Colab notebook on Github to check it out.

They were actually losing $15,000+. Most practices calculate no-show costs completely wrong. They count the missed procedure fee and stop there. But the real math? → Hygienists getting paid to do nothing → Treatment plans stalling for months → New patients who *would* show up blocked from booking → Marketing dollars wasted on patients who ghost After working with 25+ dental practices at Hex AI Agency, here's the pattern we see: The practices struggling most share 3 things: 1. They send 1 reminder (not enough) 2. They send it 24 hours before (too late) 3. They use the same message for everyone (doesn't work) The practices crushing it do this instead: 1. Multi-touch sequences (3-5 touchpoints minimum) 2. First reminder 72 hours out, not 24 3. Personalized messaging based on patient history One practice we onboarded went from 18 no-shows/week to 4. Same staff. Same patients. Different system. The uncomfortable truth? Your no-show problem isn't a patient problem. It's a communication infrastructure problem. And in 2026, that's a solved problem—if you're willing to update your approach. What's your practice's no-show rate right now? (Most owners guess 8-10%. Reality is usually 15-20%.) Understanding No-Show Patterns in Medical Appointments

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Imagine arriving at a doctor’s office only to find it eerily quiet, with empty chairs and an idle receptionist. The ripple effects of no-shows can be significant, leading to wasted resources and frustrated healthcare providers. It sparked my curiosity: why do patients miss their appointments? This project became my quest to uncover the factors behind no-shows in medical appointments.

### Why THIS Project?

My experience in medical sales exposed me to the challenges that come with missed appointments. I saw firsthand how they could affect not just patient care but also the efficiency of healthcare systems. I chose this project to explore no-show patterns with the hope of providing actionable insights that could help healthcare providers streamline their scheduling and ultimately enhance patient care.

### What Readers Will Gain:

In this article, you’ll learn about the factors influencing medical appointment no-shows, the surprising impact of SMS reminders, and insights into demographic trends. By the end, you’ll understand how simple strategies can lead to improved healthcare outcomes and better appointment management.

### Key Takeaways:

- SMS reminders significantly reduce no-show rates.
- Middle-aged patients are more likely to miss appointments.
- Certain neighborhoods are linked to higher no-show rates.
- Female patients have a higher no-show rate (13.2% vs. 6.99% for males).

### Dataset Details:

The dataset I used comes from a publicly available collection on platforms like Kaggle, encompassing over 110,000 appointments. It includes a variety of patient demographics and appointment details. Key features such as age, gender, and whether SMS reminders were sent are essential in analyzing no-show patterns. This rich dataset allowed me to explore various angles of appointment attendance, ensuring reliable and insightful analysis.

Find the Data set Here: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/joniarroba/noshowappointments/data

### Analysis Process:

My analysis journey began with cleaning and preparing the data. I organized the dataset to ensure accuracy, removing any inconsistencies that could skew results. Did you know patients need to see your practice 20 times before booking an appointment? 🤯💡 In 2026, visibility is key to earning patient trust. You’re not just showing up once—you need to be present and consistent across multiple touchpoints. Here’s what builds trust: 🦷 Social media interactions 🌐 Your website content ⭐ Positive Google reviews 📧 Emails 📣 Targeted ads The more touchpoints you have with potential patients, the more likely they are to choose you when they’re ready to book. Consistent visibility makes all the difference in making that first appointment easier to book. 👉 Follow us for more tips on building trust and increasing visibility for your practice in 2026.#PatientAcquisition#OmniDentist#DentalMarketingStrategy#MarketingWithLove#DentalPracticeGrowth#SEOForDentists#PatientTrust#VisibilityMatters

To view or add a comment, sign in

3w

The worst marketing advice I see given to med spa owners: "Just be consistent on social media and the patients will come." I've looked at dozens of med spa Instagram accounts with 2,000-5,000 followers, beautiful content, posted 3x a week for a year straight. Most of them can't point to a single patient who booked because of an Instagram post. Consistency is important. But consistent visibility without a conversion path is just content for content's sake. A patient sees your Reel. Loves it. Then what? If the answer is "they go to our bio link," you've already lost most of them. That's three clicks between interest and appointment. Each click loses 50-70% of the audience. The practices filling their schedule from social aren't just posting. They're capturing. Retargeting the people who watched. Texting the ones who clicked. Following up with the ones who inquired but didn't book. The content is just the top of the funnel, not the entire funnel.#MedSpa#MedSpaMarketing#MedSpaGrowth#AestheticMarketing#MedSpaSuccess#PatientAcquisition

Doctor Marketing Agency Cut GMB Drop-Off by 68%: Here’s How We Did It

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

## Doctor Marketing Agency

A Doctor Marketing Agency is a specialized marketing firm that focuses exclusively on helping physicians, clinics, and healthcare providers grow their practice using digital marketing strategies tailored to the healthcare industry.

Unlike generic marketing agencies, a Doctor Marketing Agency understands:

- Reputation management in a clinical setting
- Medical services terminology and search intent
- Patient behavior and healthcare decision-making patterns
- HIPAA compliance and medical privacy laws

## The Silent Killer in Local Search: GMB Drop-Off

Every day, thousands of patients search for doctors and clinics near them.

But here's the problem:

68% of medical practices were losing leads right at the doorstep.

Not because they weren’t visible on Google—but because their Google Business Profile (GMB) wasn’t converting.

Clicks were happening.

Calls were not.

And the drop-off? Massive.

At Intellius Medical, a leading Doctor Marketing Agency, we saw this pattern across dozens of clinics. So we set out to change it.

## Why This Matters: The Real Cost of GMB Drop-Off

90% of patients begin their healthcare journey online. ([Pew Research Center])

But if they click on your GMB profile and don’t call, book, or visit—your practice is invisible.

That’s why fixing GMB drop-off isn’t optional. It’s foundational to digital patient acquisition.

Here’s how our Doctor Marketing Agency tackled it head-on and slashed drop-off by 68%.

## Step 1: Analyzing Behavior, Not Just Rankings

Most agencies focus on Google rankings. But what happens after you rank?

We used heatmaps, click tracking, and scroll-depth analysis to understand patient behavior on GMB.

Key Finding:

Patients were clicking into profiles—but not taking action. Most stopped at the photo gallery or reviews.

So we restructured.

CTA #1: Want to know how patients interact with your GMB profile? [Book a free audit.]

## Step 2: Rewriting the Business Description for Conversion

The default descriptions were failing. Either too vague, too technical, or missing trust triggers.

We tested over 20 versions and found a winning format:

- Third sentence: Action ("Call today to schedule")
- Second sentence: Proof (Board-certified, 10+ years, 5-star reviews)
- First sent

# Payers shrink application window, burdening providers

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.

Jessica Boswell, RHIA, PESC

Mid Atlantic Retina•2K followers

10mo Edited

- Report this post

As if credentialing professionals didn’t already have enough hurdles… Payers are now widely adopting new protocols that only allow enrollment applications to be submitted 25–30 days before a provider’s start date - or worse, refusing applications entirely until the effective date. Yet, there’s been no improvement in processing or turnaround times. So, to be clear - Instead of hiring more staff or properly training the ones they have, payers chose to shrink the application window in an attempt to reduce their backlogs - once again shifting the burden onto providers and credentialing teams. It’s incredibly disappointing and frustrating! Physicians are the core of healthcare and should be supported so they can care for their patients, not backed into a corner by systemic payer inefficiencies and greed. This is not the solution.#Credentialing#ProviderEnrollment#HealthcareAdmin#PayerDelays#PhysicianAdvocacy#PayerEnrollment

Does the MSP office "start the process" as soon as the provider is identified? Or do they similarly "start the process" by some relative date that works backward from the target start date? 2.
